questions.question: Do the assertions in groups-awaiting-confirmation.spec.ts assert concrete DOM/state changes (visible/hidden elements, testid presence/absence) rather than tautological waits (e.g. a waitForTimeout followed by an assertion that always passes), and is there no fixed-sleep step standing in for a real poll/expect.poll on the live-updating UI, per this project's known e2e-flake precedent?

Does the new spec avoid any raw WebSocket publish to the relay for admin/invitee actions (publish-via-app rule), and does it keep the invitee's browser context open through the auto-accept assertion (closing it destroys the MLS KeyPackage, per project precedent) rather than closing and reopening it before the approval assertion?

Could this integration story's verification surface be deleted and rewritten without modifying any file outside its dependencies_allowed list (the union of its owned ACs' Spans modules: set — Outbound-join-request store, Groups page, welcomeSubscription.ts (auto-accept path))? If not, which cross-module dependencies exist and do they flow through declared seam contracts in architecture.md?

Is groups-awaiting-confirmation.spec.ts named to land in the relay bucket (groups-*.spec.ts filename convention, not accidentally run in the no-relay fast bucket — a known project e2e trap), does it use two real browser.newContext() sessions (invitee + admin) signed in through the app rather than mocked signers, and does every live-transition assertion use expect.poll/toBeVisible rather than reload()+isVisible() (a known project hydration-race trap)?

Does the spec drive the full flow described in acceptance-criteria.md's E2E Coverage section end-to-end — land on Invited banner, confirm to Awaiting, persist-across-reload, Cancel with no reload, and a second-context admin approval with no reload — through real app UI interactions only, with no production-code changes introduced by this story?

Does the spec publish every join-request/approval action through the app's real UI/publish helpers (no hand-signed raw event WebSocket.send to the relay), consistent with this project's e2e-testing rule that a raw-WebSocket-driven test can pass even when the app's own signer/publish path is broken?

Show the spec step driving a returning user opening an invite link, observing the Invited banner, tapping Request to join, observing the Awaiting banner and card, then reload()-ing and asserting the awaiting card is still present; confirm this exercises the real saveOutboundJoinRequest IndexedDB persistence path (survives reload) rather than in-memory-only mock state.

Show the spec step tapping Cancel on the awaiting card and asserting both the card and Awaiting banner are removed WITHOUT any reload() call anywhere in that assertion path; confirm the assertion uses expect.poll/toBeVisible against the live DOM, not a reload-then-check pattern that would pass even if live reactivity were broken.

Show the spec step using a second browser.newContext() signed in as the real admin to approve the join request through the app's real approval UI, and the assertion (in the requester's still-open session, no reload()) that the awaiting card disappears and the real joined-group card appears; confirm the invitee context is never closed before this assertion.

Show the data-testid attributes referenced by the spec for the awaiting banner, the awaiting card (nonce-prefix keyed, mirroring pending-request-row-*), and the Cancel action, and confirm each is asserted present in the rendered DOM at the corresponding component (not merely referenced in the spec's selector strings without a presence assertion).

Show the ordering-property test/assertion demonstrating that once a delete for a nonce has been applied to the few-outbound-join-requests store, no subsequent getSnapshot() call — including one from an async load that started before the delete but resolves after — includes a record for that nonce; confirm this is exercised against S2's real store/emitter implementation via a genuine race (e.g. triggering the async load and the delete close together), not a sequential happy-path call order that could never expose the race.

Show the assertion that across the auto-accept transition for a given nonce, no observed rendered frame shows both the awaiting card and the real joined-group card simultaneously, and no observed frame shows neither; confirm the assertion polls intermediate frames (not just before/after snapshots) so a transient double-visible or double-absent frame would actually be caught.

Show the scenario where a user Cancels (local delete) before a later-arriving admin-approval Welcome for the same nonce arrives, and the assertion that the Welcome is NOT silently dropped — it surfaces via the existing manual Accept/Decline pending-invitations path; confirm this is driven through the app's real welcomeSubscription/pending-invitations handling, not a stub that always shows the manual path regardless of the cancel-then-approve ordering.

The AC-OBS-3 test reuses nonce1's ORIGINAL join-request rumor (sent in the 'taps Request to join' test, never retracted by the later Cancel per AC-CARD-3/DD-4) rather than sending a fresh request-then-cancel pair. Does this genuinely exercise 'Cancel precedes a later-arriving admin-approval Welcome for the same nonce', or does reusing the stale pending rumor risk a different code path (e.g. an already-processed/seen-event guard) than a fresh cancel-then-approve sequence would hit?

The AC-OBS-2 poll loop (250ms interval, up to 90s) ran green on the real implementation with no observed both-visible or neither-visible frame, despite MarmotContext.tsx's onGroupJoined callback firing `void reloadGroups()` (fire-and-forget, not awaited) AFTER `deleteOutboundJoinRequest` inside welcomeSubscription.ts's auto-accept branch — a theoretical gap where the card is already gone but the real group card has not yet rendered. Is 250ms polling fine-grained enough to have a real chance of catching that gap in this app's actual IDB-read + React-commit latency, or is the gap structurally too small for browser-level Playwright polling to ever observe regardless of whether it exists?

This story discovered that groups-invite-link.spec.ts, groups-join-request-live.spec.ts, groups-approved-requester-label.spec.ts, groups-welcome.spec.ts, groups-join-request-profile.spec.ts, and groups-member-admitted-announcement.spec.ts all assert the full-screen JoinRequestCard (`getByTestId('join-request-card')`) for a NAMED, boot-identity invite-link visitor — a user who, under this epic's S3 change (groups.tsx:950's `isFreshIdentity` gate), no longer reaches that branch, since every e2e-booted identity is non-fresh at mount. Ground-truthed: groups-invite-link.spec.ts currently fails 2 of 11 tests for exactly this reason. Should these prior-epic specs be updated (by this epic or a follow-up) to assert the NEW returning-user landing (list + InviteAwaitingBanner) instead of the old full-screen replacement, before `make test-e2e-all` is run as this epic's gate?
